title: A Galaxy with Tentacles Found Far Away
summary: Scientists using the James Webb Space Telescope have found a very old galaxy that looks like a jellyfish with long tentacles. This galaxy is called a jellyfish galaxy because it has streams of gas behind it that look like tentacles. It is the farthest jellyfish galaxy ever seen, and its light took 8.5 billion years to reach Earth, so we see it as it was when the universe was young. The galaxy moves through a crowded area with hot gas, which pushes its gas back to form the tentacles. This process is called ram-pressure stripping. The galaxy has bright blue spots in its tentacles, which are new stars forming outside the main galaxy. This discovery helps us learn how galaxies changed long ago and raises questions about the early universe. The scientists studied a special area of the sky called the COSMOS field to make this find.

keywords.explanation: a huge group of stars, gas, and dust in space
long, flowing parts that look like arms, like on a jellyfish
a powerful telescope in space that takes pictures of faraway things
what we see from stars and galaxies that travels through space
when hot gas pushes on a galaxy and pulls its gas away to make tentacles
bright balls of gas in space that shine, like our sun

questions.correct_answer: It helps us learn about the early universe

background_read: This article is about a space discovery using the James Webb Space Telescope. Galaxies are huge collections of stars, and some look like jellyfish with long tentacles made of gas. Scientists study these to understand how galaxies change over time. The universe is very old, and light from faraway galaxies takes billions of years to reach us, so we see them as they were long ago. This helps us learn about the past and how things in space evolve.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists at the University of Waterloo using the James Webb Space Telescope
WHAT: Found the farthest jellyfish galaxy ever seen, with tentacles of gas and new stars
WHERE: In deep space, in a region called the COSMOS field, far from the Milky Way
WHY: To learn how galaxies were reshaped in the early universe and understand conditions billions of years ago

perspectives.description: This finding shows how new telescopes like JWST can reveal unknown galaxies and help us explore the distant past.
